evidence: The rainy red-light setting, held confession, long verse/compact chorus shape, repeated hook, second-chorus ceiling, and silent coda all trace directly to the bound TrackSpec and C006 fingerprint.
The current output has the exact three paste-ready fenced fields in order, keeps the title outside them, and confines structural tags and lyric text to the Lyrics block.
The restrained 78 BPM pulse, nylon-guitar and muted-piano palette, near-field low-register vocal, minor-subdominant chorus color, and gentle coda all support the suspended red-light mood.
The title is dramatized by the recurring 'Stay red a little longer' hook, while the changed final response, 'I'll leave when the light turns green,' resolves the central image and narrative.
The English uses natural syntax, concrete verbs, controlled clause lengths, and no syntactic break is introduced by 'This time I pause before I read your message.'

All six bound sections appear in the required Intro-Verse-Chorus-Verse-Chorus-Coda order; both instrumental sections are lyric-free, both choruses are written in full, and the lyric load passes duration-readiness validation.

The Basic Prompt contains positive audible direction, while the exclusion field is limited to disallowed accompaniment, vocal, and dynamic traits without contradicting the requested jazz-alto vocal identity.

The track shares the playlist's rainy night-drive, narrow-dynamic K-pop ballad language while distinguishing itself through the C006 letter-refrain form, red-light suspension, nylon-guitar entry, subtle hook level, and instrumental coda.

The breakup message explains the narrator's reluctance to go home and desire for the light to remain red; taking the long river route then extends that same avoidance rather than introducing a new motive.
Almost calling leads to the deliberate choice to keep both hands on the wheel; rereading the message at the next stop leads to accepting the ending and finishing the drive alone.

The narrator's choices stay true to a recently separated person seeking a brief delay: rereading a final message while stopped, taking a longer route, nearly calling, and postponing home are all emotionally prepared by the opening verse.

The shift from asking for more red-light time to promising to leave on green is earned by the intervening restraint, second stopped intersection, rereading, and conscious pause; it is a modest internal decision rather than an abrupt recovery.

The sequence progresses coherently from a nearly-two-a.m. red light after leaving the addressee's place, to a southbound river detour, a closing gas station, a bridge, and the next stopped intersection.
The phone remains face down on the passenger seat between the first and second stops, and 'This time' clearly marks the later rereading rather than an unexplained temporal reset.
A single first-person driver narrates throughout, and every second-person reference points consistently to the former partner who sent the final message.
The choruses preserve the same situation and addressee; the narrator's stance changes from delay to acceptance without any role or viewpoint shift.
'The wipers clear the glass, then rain returns' literally establishes the repeating visual obstruction and supports the narrator's inability to obtain lasting emotional clarity.
'Then keep my hands at ten and two instead' has a clear literal action and context-supported meaning: the narrator rejects the impulse to call while driving.
'This time I pause before I read your message' is a literal, context-supported action at the second stopped intersection and prepares the following realization.
'I'll leave when the light turns green' literally completes the stopped-car action and semantically converts the repeated red-light plea into a bounded decision to continue.
